description:
You are an integration-developer who has just received a mock JSON reply from a remote optimisation-solver API.  
Your goal is to check that the payload indicates a successful (i.e. “optimal”) solve and then write a very small status log that other automated systems will ingest later.

Environment layout (already present when you start the task)
1. Directory: /home/user/api_test
2. File: /home/user/api_test/solver_response.json  
   – This is the raw API reply you must inspect.  
   – You may freely read this file but you must not edit or delete it.

Task to perform
A. Read /home/user/api_test/solver_response.json and extract  
   • the value of the top-level key “status” (expected string)  
   • the value of the top-level key “objective_value” (expected number)  
   • the entire “x” array (expected list of numbers, keep order)  

B. Create a brand-new text file called  
   /home/user/api_test/optimization_test.log  

C. Write exactly three lines to that log file, **in the precise order and format shown below** (final newline after the third line is mandatory):

Line 1: STATUS: <status_value>  
Line 2: OBJECTIVE: <objective_value>  
Line 3: VARIABLES: <comma-separated-x-values>

Formatting rules the automated checker relies on
1. Upper-case keys (STATUS, OBJECTIVE, VARIABLES) followed by a single colon and a single space.  
2. No extra spaces anywhere else (e.g. no space after commas inside the VARIABLES list).  
3. The VARIABLES line must list the numbers exactly as they appear in the JSON array, separated only by commas.  
4. All three lines must end with Unix LF ( \n ).  

Allowed tools  
You may use any standard Linux shell utilities (e.g. jq, cat, awk, sed) or short Python/Node interpreters—whatever you prefer—so long as you achieve the exact file and content described above.  

Typical session footprint  
A correct solution normally needs no more than 3-4 terminal commands:  
• one command to read or parse the JSON,  
• one or two commands to build the three output lines,  
• one command to write them into /home/user/api_test/optimization_test.log.  

When you are done you can display the file’s contents to verify they match the required format, but that final cat (or similar) is optional.
truth:
Initial files before the agent starts:
Path: /home/user/api_test/solver_response.json  
Contents (with trailing newline):
{
  "status": "optimal",
  "x": [1.5, 0.0, 2.5],
  "objective_value": -7.25,
  "solver_time_sec": 0.003
}

Target file the grader will look for:
Path: /home/user/api_test/optimization_test.log  
Exact required contents (including terminating newline characters):

STATUS: optimal
OBJECTIVE: -7.25
VARIABLES: 1.5,0.0,2.5

Note: There is a final newline after “2.5”.
name: task_000000_a34927e4
